Results from this study indicate that mulch decomposition can account for up to 30% of RTOT.

nodes.name: Recovery of net carbon sink function

source_spans.text: Mulch decomposition impedes recovery of net carbon sink function in a restored peatland
Price (1997) determined that applying straw mulch to the surface of bare peat increased the surface soil moisture
Price et al. (1998) and Petrone et al. (2001) estimated that the evaporation from mulched covered sites was 80% of that of the bare peat rate
Price (1997) determined that applying straw mulch to the surface of bare peat increased the surface soil moisture
Price et al. (1998) and Petrone et al. (2001) estimated that the evaporation from mulched covered sites was 80% of that of the bare peat rate

the rate of straw decomposition has been shown to decrease with reductions in pore water (Summerell and Burgress, 1989) and soil moisture content (Leiros et al., 1999), and increase with increasing peat temperature (Summerell and Burgress, 1989).

the rate of straw decomposition has been shown to decrease with reductions in pore water (Summerell and Burgress, 1989) and soil moisture content (Leiros et al., 1999), and increase with increasing peat temperature (Summerell and Burgress, 1989).
Due to the decomposition of this mulch layer over the first several seasons of restoration, the peatland became a larger source of CO2
Due to the decomposition of this mulch layer over the first several seasons of restoration, the peatland became a larger source of CO2
Moist conditions tended to display the greatest CO2 production rates for both new and old straw under the various incubation temperatures.
Moist conditions tended to display the greatest CO2 production rates for both new and old straw under the various incubation temperatures.
Under all moisture regimes and incubation temperatures, new straw had greater CO2 production and thus straw mulch decomposition, compared with old straw mulch
Under all moisture regimes and incubation temperatures, new straw had greater CO2 production and thus straw mulch decomposition, compared with old straw mulch
However, peatland restoration has recently been shown to enhance carbon sequestration on abandoned cutover peatlands

This is carried out by blocking the drainage ditches to raise the water table and rewet the surface
